area: The Claude desktop / claude.ai app interface and feature set (UI specification for WorkCrew to mirror with purple branding)
summary: The Claude app (claude.ai web and the Electron desktop wrapper) uses a calm, dense, editorial layout: a narrow persistent left sidebar for navigation (New chat, Search, Projects/Artifacts, Recents, account/plan footer), an optional top mode area (Chat / Cowork / Code in the newer desktop builds), and a vertically centered empty state with the greeting "How can I help you today?" plus a single rounded composer. The composer carries a "+" attachment control on the lower left and the model name plus effort level next to the send button on the lower right; clicking the model name opens model choices and a four-level effort selector (Low / Medium / High [Default] / Max) with an Extended Thinking toggle. Below the composer in the empty state sit quick-start chips (Write, Learn, Code, Life stuff, and Claude's choice) that expand into rotating task templates. The aesthetic is warm and minimal: serif typography on content surfaces, borders instead of shadows, no avatars, a single warm-orange accent (~#C96442 / #D97757), and dark surfaces around #262624 with slightly raised panels near #30302E. WorkCrew should keep the same structure and density but swap the orange accent for purple, shrink oversized headings, and tighten vertical spacing. Note: claude.ai itself is auth-gated so these findings come from Anthropic support docs, the Mobbin/brand pages, and a documented open-source Claude clone, not a live capture.
findings: LEFT SIDEBAR (persistent, narrow ~260px, darker than main panel): top action is 'New chat' (with the orange sparkle/asterisk star icon), followed by 'Search'/chat search. Then navigation entries for 'Projects' and a dedicated 'Artifacts' space ('Artifacts has a new home in the sidebar'; it is its own browsable space to view all creations and examples). Below that is the 'Recents' list of recent conversations (chronological, single-line truncated titles). The sidebar footer holds the account / plan area (user name, plan tier, settings access). Source: support.claude.com get-started + artifacts articles.

TOP MODE TABS (newer desktop builds): the Claude desktop app exposes three top-level modes: 'Chat' (normal conversations), 'Cowork' (Dispatch / longer agentic work), and 'Code' (software development). The April 2026 Claude Code desktop redesign added a session-management sidebar with filtering by status/project/environment and a drag-and-drop multi-pane workspace (chat, diff, preview, terminal, file editor). For WorkCrew's chat surface, the relevant analog is the Chat empty state, with optional mode tabs if you want Cowork/Code parity.

EMPTY STATE (new chat): vertically centered. A single centered greeting heading 'How can I help you today?' paired with the orange sparkle icon. Directly under it sits the composer. Under the composer sit the quick-start chips. There is NO sidebar in the open-source clone empty state, but the real app keeps the sidebar and centers only the main column. Keep the greeting modest in size (it is a single line, not an oversized hero) and rely on whitespace + the chips, not large type, to fill the space.

COMPOSER: a single minimal bordered input with large rounded corners (clone uses rounded-2xl ~16px radius), border-only definition (no drop shadow), composer surface slightly lighter than page background (dark: ~#1F1E1B composer on ~#2B2A27 page). Lower-left holds the '+' attachment button (Anthropic docs: 'click the + button in the lower left or type / to view additional options and commands'). Lower-right holds the model name + effort label and the send/submit button. Voice input control sits in the composer control row. In chat state the composer becomes sticky at the bottom with a fade-out gradient overlay above it.

MODEL + REASONING-EFFORT SELECTOR: 'The selected model and effort level appear next to the send button.' Clicking the model name opens the picker with a 'More models' expansion. Effort is a sub-control with four levels: Low, Medium, High (labeled 'Default'), and Max, available for the top models (Opus 4.8/4.7/4.6, Sonnet 4.6). Extended Thinking is a toggle reached by hovering 'Effort' then toggling 'Thinking' (older models show a separate 'Extended' toggle). When thinking is active a 'Thinking' indicator with a timer and an expandable reasoning section appears in the response. Model/effort/thinking can be changed mid-conversation and apply to the next response. Source: support.claude.com article 8664678.

QUICK-START CHIPS (empty state): five categories rendered as thin-bordered, transparent-background chips: 'Write', 'Learn', 'Code', 'Life stuff', and 'Claude's choice'. Clicking a chip reveals a rotating set of concrete task templates that change each session (e.g. Write -> case studies, content calendars, social posts; Learn -> discussion prompts, knowledge maps; Code -> 'vibe code with me', 'turn a screenshot into working code'; Life stuff -> organize space, sleep habits, exercise routines). The open-source clone variant shows chips like Write / Learn / Code / From Drive / From Calendar, confirming the thin-border, transparent, pill style.

ARTIFACTS / SPLIT VIEW: when Claude creates an artifact it opens in 'a dedicated window to the right of the main chat', producing a split-screen layout (chat left, artifact right). The artifact window has controls in its lower-right corner for view-code / copy / download, a version selector to switch between iterations, and chat controls (a slider icon in the upper right) to switch between multiple artifacts in one conversation. A dedicated Artifacts space in the sidebar lists all artifacts. Mirror this as a right-docked panel for WorkCrew's generated outputs/previews.

VISUAL LANGUAGE / DENSITY: warm and editorial, not the cold neutral-gray generic-AI look. Borders define surfaces ('no shadows, no avatars'); panels differ by 1-2 steps of luminance rather than elevation shadows. Serif typography is used on content surfaces for an editorial feel (the clone uses font-serif on every surface; message body line-height ~1.65rem / leading-[1.65rem] for comfortable reading rhythm). Spacing is generous-but-tight: comfortable line height with restrained heading sizes. The single accent color is reserved for the sparkle icon, send button, and active states; everything else is neutral.

COLOR TOKENS (dark theme, from clone + brand pages; treat as close approximations): page background ~#2B2A27 (warm near-black), raised/composer surface ~#1F1E1B, borders ~#3D3A35, accent orange ~#C96442 (brand variants cited: #D97757, #C15F3C 'Crail', #DA7756). Light theme: background #F0ECE0, composer white, borders #E5E0D6. Anthropic brand neutrals cited include Pampas #F4F3EE and Cloudy #B1ADA1. For WorkCrew: keep these warm dark neutrals (or shift very slightly cooler) and replace the orange accent token with a single purple accent used identically (sparkle icon, send button, active nav item, focus rings).

HOW WORKCREW DIVERGES AND FIXES: the current WorkCrew screenshot has oversized headings and a sparse layout. Fixes: (1) reduce the greeting to a single modest-weight line (do not use a giant hero heading); (2) tighten vertical rhythm - move composer and chips up so the empty state reads as a centered cluster, not three far-apart blocks; (3) add the persistent left sidebar (New chat, Search, Projects, Artifacts, Recents, account footer) to fill horizontal space and add density; (4) put model + effort + send together at the composer's lower right and '+' at lower left to match muscle memory; (5) adopt border-only surfaces (drop heavy shadows), warm dark neutrals, and serif body text; (6) reserve a single purple accent and apply it exactly where Claude uses orange.
recommendations: Build the chat shell as: persistent left sidebar (260px) + centered main column. Sidebar order: New chat (with purple sparkle), Search, Projects, Artifacts, Recents list, account/plan footer pinned to bottom.

Implement the empty state as a single vertically centered cluster: one-line greeting 'How can I help you today?' (modest size, e.g. 24-28px, not a hero), the rounded composer directly under it, then the chip row. Do not spread these across the viewport.

Composer: one bordered, ~16px-radius input, surface 1-2 luminance steps above the page, no shadow. Lower-left '+' attachment + '/' command affordance; lower-right model-name+effort label, voice button, and send button in the brand purple.

Model/effort control: clicking the model name opens a popover with model list ('More models') and a four-level effort selector Low / Medium / High (Default) / Max plus an Extended Thinking toggle; allow changing mid-conversation, applied to the next turn.

Quick-start chips: render Write / Learn / Code / Life stuff / Claude's-choice equivalents as thin-bordered transparent pills that expand into rotating task templates. Rebrand 'Claude's choice' to a WorkCrew-appropriate label.

Artifacts/output: open generated artifacts/previews in a right-docked split panel with copy/download/view-code controls in its lower-right and a version selector; add an Artifacts entry in the sidebar listing all outputs.

Adopt a warm dark neutral palette (page ~#2B2A27, surface ~#1F1E1B, border ~#3D3A35) with a single purple accent replacing #C96442 everywhere the orange is used; define these as CSS variables / design tokens.

Use serif for message/content body with line-height ~1.65 and a sans for UI chrome; favor border-defined surfaces over shadows, remove avatars, and tighten spacing to raise density toward the Claude reference.
risks: claude.ai is authentication-gated and could not be captured live; the exact pixel values, paddings, and current sidebar order may differ slightly from these doc-derived and clone-derived approximations. Validate against a live logged-in capture before finalizing.

Color hex values vary across sources (#C96442 vs #D97757 vs #C15F3C vs #DA7756); these are close-but-not-canonical. WorkCrew is rebranding to purple anyway, so use the structure and luminance relationships rather than copying Anthropic's exact orange.
The Chat/Cowork/Code mode tabs and the multi-pane workspace are from the April 2026 Claude Code desktop redesign and may not all apply to a plain chat product; adopt selectively to avoid scope creep.

Cloning Anthropic's look too closely risks brand/trade-dress concerns; the purple rebrand, distinct logo/wordmark, and renamed chips ('Claude's choice') are important to keep WorkCrew clearly its own product.

area: Claude Code agentic runs and scheduled "routines" — UX patterns for WorkCrew (presenting automation runs and user-created recurring routines for non-developers)
summary: Claude Code splits the problem into two layers WorkCrew should mirror. (1) An agentic RUN is presented as a live, step-by-step session: a permission mode sets how much it asks, plan mode shows a proposed plan the user approves before anything touches disk, a todo/task list with a sticky progress bar shows step progress in real time, and per-action approval prompts gate edits and commands (with an "always allow" memory). (2) A ROUTINE is a saved configuration (prompt + scope + connectors + triggers) that runs automatically. Anthropic ships two distinct flavors under one "Routines" page: cloud Routines (run on Anthropic infra even when the machine is off; min interval 1 hour; triggers can be schedule, API/webhook, or GitHub event; no approval prompts during a run) and local Desktop scheduled tasks (run on your machine, min interval 1 minute, configurable per-task permissions, access to local files, fire a desktop notification, and surface under a "Scheduled" sidebar section). Both share a creation form (Name, Description/Instructions, Schedule, model/permission pickers), preset schedules (Manual/Hourly/Daily/Weekdays/Weekly) with natural-language for custom cron, a detail page with Run now, Active/Paused toggle, Edit, and a run-history list, and a rule that each run opens as a full reviewable session. For WorkCrew (non-developers automating PC tasks via pywinauto/Playwright), the right model is: local-machine routines as the default (since PC automation needs the local desktop), preset-first scheduling with plain-language custom, a clear pre-run plan/approval step, a live run timeline with task checklist, desktop notifications, a Routines list with pause/run-now/history, and a missed-run catch-up policy.
findings: TWO-LAYER MODEL: Claude Code cleanly separates (a) how a single agentic run is presented and approved from (b) how recurring routines are configured and scheduled. WorkCrew should keep the same separation: a 'Run' view (live execution) and a 'Routines' view (saved schedules). Source: code.claude.com/docs/en/routines and /desktop-scheduled-tasks.

PERMISSION MODES set the run's oversight level. Claude Code has default (asks before edits/commands, reads-only auto-approved), acceptEdits (auto-approves edits and common filesystem ops), plan (research only, no edits), auto (executes with a background safety classifier, few prompts), dontAsk (only pre-approved tools), bypassPermissions (no checks). For WorkCrew, the user-facing analog is a per-routine setting like 'Ask me before each action' vs 'Run automatically' vs 'Plan first, then I approve'. Source: /permission-modes.

PLAN MODE is the key pre-run approval UX: Claude reads/explores but makes no changes, then presents a plan and asks how to proceed with options 'Approve and run automatically', 'Approve and accept edits', 'Approve and review each step', 'Keep planning with feedback', or refine. Approving names the session from the plan. Ctrl+G opens the plan in an editor to edit before running. WorkCrew should show non-developers a plain-language plan ('Here is what I will do: 1... 2... 3...') with Approve / Edit / Cancel before any clicks/typing happen on their PC.

STEP-BY-STEP PROGRESS is shown via a todo/task list. Newer Claude Code (v2.1.142+, Agent SDK 0.3.142+) uses structured Task tools (TaskCreate/TaskUpdate/TaskGet/TaskList) replacing the older TodoWrite; items have states pending/in_progress/completed and render as a checklist with a sticky progress bar showing completion percentage and the active task. WorkCrew should render a live checklist + progress bar during a run so non-developers can watch each automation step tick off.

PER-ACTION APPROVAL with memory: in modes that prompt, each risky action (edit, command) pauses for Yes/No, and choosing 'always allow' records the approval so future runs of that routine auto-approve the same tool without prompting. Approvals are reviewable and revocable from the task detail page ('Always allowed' panel). WorkCrew should let users approve a step once and remember it per-routine, with a panel to review/revoke granted permissions.

CLOUD ROUTINES (the headline 'Routines' feature, research preview on Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise) = a saved config of prompt + repositories + environment + connectors + triggers, run on Anthropic-managed infra so it works with the laptop closed. Managed at claude.ai/code/routines or via /schedule in CLI. Triggers: Scheduled, API (per-routine HTTPS endpoint + bearer token, POST to /fire with optional freeform 'text' payload), and GitHub events (pull_request, release, with filters). A single routine can combine triggers. Source: /routines.

CLOUD ROUTINE SCHEDULING: presets are hourly/daily/weekdays/weekly, plus one-off at a future timestamp; custom cron is set via '/schedule update' in CLI. MINIMUM INTERVAL IS 1 HOUR (more frequent expressions are rejected). Times entered in local zone, converted automatically. Runs are deliberately staggered a few minutes (deterministic offset per routine). One-off runs auto-disable after firing and the UI marks them 'Ran'. Source: /routines.

DESKTOP SCHEDULED TASKS (LOCAL) are the better analog for WorkCrew's PC automation. Created from the same Desktop 'Routines' page via New routine > Local (vs Remote for cloud). They run on the user's machine with access to local files and tools, MINIMUM INTERVAL 1 MINUTE, configurable per-task permission mode, and only fire while the app is open and the computer awake. Fields: Name (kebab-cased to a folder), Description (shown in list), Instructions (with permission-mode + model pickers, working folder, optional isolated worktree toggle), Schedule. Source: /desktop-scheduled-tasks.

LOCAL SCHEDULE PRESETS: Manual (only Run now), Hourly, Daily (time picker, defaults 9:00 AM local), Weekdays (skips weekends), Weekly (time + day picker). For anything the picker lacks (every 15 min, first of month, one-off future), the user just asks in natural language ('schedule a task to run all tests every 6 hours' / 'remind me at 3pm tomorrow'). One-time tasks auto-disable after firing. This 'presets + plain-language for advanced' pattern is exactly right for non-developers.

RUN PRESENTATION FOR SCHEDULED TASKS: when a task fires, the user gets a DESKTOP NOTIFICATION and a new session appears under a 'Scheduled' section in the sidebar; opening it shows what Claude did, the changes, and any pending permission prompts. Each run is a full, reviewable session. Cloud routine runs likewise each create a session you open to 'watch the run in real time, review changes'. WorkCrew: every routine run should produce a reviewable run record/session, surfaced via notification.

MONITORING / RUN HISTORY: the routine detail page lists past runs; clicking a run opens the full session transcript. A green status means the session started and exited without infra error, NOT that the task succeeded — users must open the run to confirm. Skipped runs are shown with hover reasons (computer asleep, previous run still running, other tasks running). 'Show more' loads older entries. WorkCrew should show run history with honest status (succeeded vs ran-with-issues vs skipped-with-reason), not a misleading green check.

PAUSE / EDIT / CONTROL: from a routine/task detail page users can Run now (fire immediately), toggle Active/Paused (pauses scheduled runs without deleting; paused routines keep config), Edit (instructions, schedule, folder, model, connectors, triggers), Review allowed permissions, and Delete (which archives the routine's sessions; an optional checkbox also deletes on-disk files). Routines can be managed conversationally too ('pause my dependency-audit task'). WorkCrew needs the same first-class controls per routine.

MISSED-RUN / CATCH-UP POLICY (local tasks): on app start or wake, Desktop checks for runs missed in the last 7 days and starts EXACTLY ONE catch-up run for the most recently missed time, discarding older ones, with a notification. Because timing can drift (a 9am task may run at 11pm), Anthropic advises putting timing guardrails in the prompt itself. WorkCrew should adopt a single-catch-up-on-wake policy and warn users that local routines only fire while the app/PC is on.

AGENT VIEW = the multi-run monitoring dashboard ('claude agents'): one screen listing every background session grouped by state — Pinned, Ready for review, Needs input, Working, Completed — each row showing name, current activity, and time since last change. State icons are color/animation coded: Working (animated), Needs input (yellow), Idle (dimmed), Completed (green), Failed (red), Stopped (grey). Users 'peek' (Space) to see latest output or the question without the full transcript, reply inline, or 'attach' for the full conversation. WorkCrew's run dashboard should group runs by state with these same statuses and a peek-vs-open interaction. Source: /agent-view.

SELF-CONTAINED PROMPT GUIDANCE: because routines run autonomously and cannot ask clarifying questions, Anthropic stresses the instruction must define success and what to do with results (e.g. 'Review open PRs labeled needs-review, comment on issues, post a summary to #eng-reviews'). For WorkCrew non-developers, the creation flow should help users write a complete instruction, ideally via a guided/templated prompt builder or by capturing a demonstrated workflow, rather than a blank text box.

SCOPING / SAFETY surface: cloud routines scope capability via selected repos (branch-push setting), environment (network access levels Trusted/Custom/Full, env vars/secrets, cached setup script), and included connectors (all connected MCPs included by default; remove unneeded ones since every tool including writes runs without asking). Admins can disable routines org-wide. WorkCrew's local analog: scope each routine to specific apps/folders/connectors and a network policy, default to least privilege, and make clear that a routine acts 'as you'.
recommendations: Adopt the two-surface architecture: a live 'Run' view (timeline of steps with a checklist + progress bar, current action, and inline approval prompts) and a 'Routines' library (list of saved schedules with status). Reuse the same run-session record for both ad-hoc runs and scheduled-routine runs so history is uniform.

Default WorkCrew routines to LOCAL execution (the Desktop-scheduled-task model), because PC automation via pywinauto/Playwright needs the live desktop, local files, and the user's logged-in apps. Cloud-style 'runs even when PC is off' is not achievable for desktop GUI automation; be explicit in the UI that routines fire only while WorkCrew is running and the PC is awake.

Offer a plain-language permission setting per routine instead of exposing raw modes: e.g. 'Plan first, I approve' (plan mode), 'Ask me before each action' (default), and 'Run automatically' (acceptEdits/auto). Always show a pre-run plan for the first run of any new routine so non-developers see exactly what will be clicked/typed before it happens.

Implement a real-time run timeline backed by a task list (mirror Claude Code's Task tools: create/update/get/list with pending/in_progress/completed states) and a sticky progress bar with percentage and the active step. Each step should show a human-readable label ('Open Outlook', 'Export report to Downloads').

Use preset-first scheduling: Manual, Hourly, Daily (default time, e.g. 9:00 AM local), Weekdays, Weekly (day + time), plus a 'one-off at a future time' option that auto-disables after firing. Provide custom cron behind a plain-language input ('every 15 minutes', 'first of each month') rather than asking non-developers to write cron. Store and run in local time, with a small deterministic stagger to avoid thundering herd.

Give every run a desktop notification and surface runs under a dedicated 'Scheduled'/'Runs' section. Each run must be openable as a full reviewable record showing the steps taken, screenshots/diffs of what changed, and any prompt the run is waiting on.

Build a routine detail page with first-class controls: Run now, Active/Paused toggle (preserve config when paused), Edit (instructions, schedule, scope, model), an 'Always allowed' permissions panel that is reviewable and revocable, run history, and Delete (with a clear note about what data is removed).

Show honest run status, not just a green check. Distinguish 'completed successfully', 'ran but with issues/blocked action', and 'skipped' (with reason such as PC was asleep, prior run still in progress, or app closed). This directly mirrors Anthropic's warning that green only means the session started/exited cleanly.

Adopt a missed-run catch-up policy: on app launch/wake, run at most one catch-up for the most recently missed occurrence (discard older), notify the user, and encourage timing guardrails in the routine's instructions for time-sensitive tasks.

Provide a multi-run dashboard (analogous to agent view) grouping runs by state — Needs input, Working, Completed, Failed/Skipped — with at-a-glance status icons (animated=working, yellow=needs input, green=done, red=failed, grey=stopped) and a 'peek' interaction to see the latest step or the pending question without opening the full transcript.

Help non-developers author complete, self-contained routine instructions: offer templates ('Daily: download my bank statement and file it', 'Every Monday: compile last week's report'), a guided prompt builder, and ideally a 'record once, replay on schedule' capture mode, since autonomous runs cannot ask clarifying questions mid-run.

Scope each routine to least privilege: explicit list of allowed apps, folders, and connectors, plus a network policy. Make clear in the UI that a routine acts with the user's identity and access, and provide an org/admin-style global kill switch to disable all routines.
risks: Desktop GUI automation (pywinauto/Playwright) fundamentally cannot run 'with the PC off' the way cloud Routines do. Do not over-promise cloud-style always-on behavior; WorkCrew routines depend on the machine being awake and the app running, which constrains reliability vs Anthropic's cloud routines.

Autonomous runs that act on the live desktop without per-step approval are higher risk than code edits (they can send emails, move money, click destructive buttons). Anthropic gates this with plan mode + a safety classifier (auto mode) + protected paths + boundary statements; WorkCrew has no equivalent classifier yet, so defaulting to 'ask before each action' or 'plan first' is safer than an unattended 'run automatically' mode.

A green/'completed' status is misleading: it only signals the session ran, not that the goal was achieved. If WorkCrew copies a naive success indicator, non-developers will trust failed automations. Build outcome verification or at least an explicit 'review this run' nudge.

Schedule timing can drift badly when the machine sleeps (a 9am task running at 11pm). Without catch-up rules and prompt-level guardrails, scheduled PC tasks can fire at surprising times and take unintended actions.

Min-interval and stagger matter: Claude Code rejects sub-hour cloud schedules and staggers runs to protect infrastructure. WorkCrew allowing very frequent local runs (sub-minute) could overload the PC or collide with the user's foreground work; enforce a sensible minimum and avoid running while the user is actively at the keyboard.

Routines run 'as the user' with their connected accounts and full local access. A poorly scoped or prompt-injected routine could exfiltrate data or take harmful actions. Least-privilege scoping per routine and a global disable switch are essential, and credential/permission storage must be secure.

Research-preview status: Anthropic's routines API surface, beta headers, limits, and even the Task vs TodoWrite tooling are explicitly changing. Any WorkCrew design that imitates current Claude Code internals should treat those details as moving targets and not hard-code against them.

area: The Anthropic Messages API for a streaming chat + agent app (WorkCrew): endpoint, SSE streaming, multi-turn, tool-use loop, PDF/image input, prompt caching, model ids and pricing, token counting, max_tokens, and a server-side Node/TypeScript integration pattern.
summary: Everything in a Claude chat/agent app goes through a single endpoint, POST https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages. It is stateless: you resend the full conversation each turn. For WorkCrew, the API key MUST live only in the Fastify backend; the Electron renderer talks to your backend, never to api.anthropic.com directly. Use the official @anthropic-ai/sdk in both the backend and any shared Zod-contract layer (never raw fetch in a TS project). Default model for launch quality is claude-opus-4-8 (Opus 4.8, 1M context, 128K max output, $5/$25 per MTok); use claude-sonnet-4-6 for the fast/cheap balance ($3/$15) and claude-haiku-4-5 for cheapest/fastest ($1/$5, 200K context). Stream every chat turn over SSE and proxy those bytes to the renderer so users see incremental text. The tool-use loop (tool_use blocks out, tool_result blocks back) is the basis for WorkCrew's agent actions (pywinauto/Playwright wrappers become custom tools). PDFs go in as document content blocks (base64, url, or Files API file_id); images as image blocks. Prompt caching (cache_control: ephemeral) cuts cost ~90% on the repeated system prompt and tool list. Count tokens with the count_tokens endpoint, never tiktoken.", "findings will continue in the findings array"
findings: ENDPOINT + HEADERS: All chat and agent traffic is POST https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages. Required headers: x-api-key: <key>, anthropic-version: 2023-06-01, content-type: application/json. Beta features add anthropic-beta (e.g. files-api-2025-04-14 for the Files API). The endpoint is STATELESS — there is no conversation id; you resend the entire messages[] array every turn. Supporting endpoints: POST /v1/messages/count_tokens, POST /v1/messages/batches (50% cheaper, async), POST /v1/files (upload PDFs/images once, reference by file_id), GET /v1/models and GET /v1/models/{id} (live capability/pricing/context-window discovery).

MINIMAL REQUEST SHAPE: { "model": "claude-opus-4-8", "max_tokens": 16000, "system": "You are WorkCrew...", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}] }. max_tokens is REQUIRED. Rules: messages[] must be non-empty, the first message must be role:user, roles otherwise represent turns (consecutive same-role messages are merged). content is either a plain string or an array of content blocks (text, image, document, tool_use, tool_result).

RESPONSE SHAPE: { "id": "msg_...", "type": "message", "role": "assistant", "model": "claude-opus-4-8", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "..."}], "stop_reason": "end_turn", "stop_details": null, "usage": {"input_tokens": N, "output_tokens": N, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 0, "cache_read_input_tokens": 0} }. content is an array of blocks — for the chat UI, iterate and render text blocks; in TS you must narrow on block.type === 'text' before reading block.text. stop_reason values: end_turn (normal), max_tokens (hit the cap — raise it or stream), tool_use (model wants a tool — run it and continue the loop), pause_turn (server-tool loop paused — resend to resume), refusal (safety decline — check stop_details, do not read content[0] blindly), stop_sequence.

SSE STREAMING (core of the chat UX): call the streaming variant (SDK client.messages.stream(...) in TS) or set "stream": true on raw HTTP. The server emits Server-Sent Events in this order: message_start (message metadata, empty content), then per content block: content_block_start, one-or-more content_block_delta, content_block_stop; then message_delta (carries final stop_reason and cumulative usage.output_tokens), then message_stop. To render incremental text: on content_block_delta where delta.type === 'text_delta', append delta.text to the live message. thinking blocks stream as thinking_delta (delta.thinking); tool inputs stream as input_json_delta. Always stream any turn with large input/output or high max_tokens — non-streaming requests above ~16K output risk SDK/HTTP timeouts.

RAW SSE WIRE FORMAT (for proxying to the Electron renderer): each event is `event: <type>
` followed by `data: <json>

`. Example deltas: event: content_block_delta / data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":"Hello"}}. The simplest WorkCrew pattern: backend opens the Anthropic stream, and the Fastify route re-emits these same SSE frames (or a simplified {type, text} envelope) down to the renderer over its own SSE/WebSocket; the renderer appends text_delta chunks to the active assistant bubble.

MULTI-TURN + SYSTEM PROMPTS: Because the API is stateless, WorkCrew must persist the messages[] array (libSQL/SQLite) and resend it each turn. The system prompt is a top-level `system` field (string, or an array of text blocks for cache_control). Append the assistant's full response.content (not just the text) back into messages as a {role:'assistant', content: response.content} turn, then add the next {role:'user', ...}. Preserving full content blocks matters for tool_use and (on thinking models) thinking blocks. Keep the system prompt BYTE-FROZEN across requests (no interpolated timestamps/user-ids) so prompt caching works.

TOOL-USE LOOP (basis for WorkCrew agent actions): 1) Define tools[] — each tool is {name, description, input_schema (JSON Schema)}. Write descriptions prescriptively ('Call this when the user asks to click X'), and use enums for fixed value sets. 2) Send the request with tools and optional tool_choice ({type:'auto'} default, {type:'any'} force a tool, {type:'tool', name} force one, {type:'none'} forbid). 3) If stop_reason === 'tool_use', the response.content contains one or more tool_use blocks {type:'tool_use', id:'toolu_...', name, input}. 4) Execute each tool, then send a NEW request with the assistant turn appended AND a user turn containing tool_result blocks: {type:'tool_result', tool_use_id: <matching id>, content: <result>, is_error?: true}. 5) Repeat until stop_reason === 'end_turn'. Each tool_result MUST carry the matching tool_use_id; send all results from a multi-tool response in one user message. For WorkCrew, wrap pywinauto and Playwright actions as custom tools (e.g. click_element, type_text, navigate) — dedicated tools let the backend gate/confirm destructive actions before executing.

TOOL RUNNER vs MANUAL LOOP (TS): the @anthropic-ai/sdk ships a beta tool runner (client.beta.messages.toolRunner(...) with betaZodTool + Zod schemas) that runs the whole loop automatically and returns the final message. Use it for simple tools. Use the MANUAL loop (loop until stop_reason==='end_turn', append full content, match tool_use_id) when WorkCrew needs human-in-the-loop confirmation, custom logging, or conditional execution before running a side-effecting desktop/browser action — which it will, for safety. SERVER-SIDE TOOLS (web_search_20260209, web_fetch_20260209, code_execution_20260120) run on Anthropic infra and can return stop_reason:'pause_turn' after 10 internal iterations — resend the user+assistant turns (no extra 'Continue' message) to resume; cap continuations (~5) to avoid loops.

PDF / DOCUMENT INPUT (verified live): send a document content block in a user message. Three source types: (a) base64 — {"type":"document","source":{"type":"base64","media_type":"application/pdf","data":"<base64>"}}; (b) url — source:{type:'url', url:'https://...'}; (c) Files API — source:{type:'file', file_id:'file_...'} (needs anthropic-beta: files-api-2025-04-14). PDF processing uses vision: each page is rendered to an image AND text-extracted, so Claude reads charts/tables/layout, not just text. Limits: max request size 32 MB (entire payload), max 600 pages/request (100 pages for 200K-context models like Haiku). Cost ~1,500-3,000 text tokens/page plus image tokens per page. Place the document block BEFORE the text question. Add cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} on the document block to cache it across repeated queries. For WorkCrew, upload reused PDFs via the Files API and reference by file_id to keep payloads small.

IMAGE / VISION INPUT: image content block, two source types: base64 — {"type":"image","source":{"type":"base64","media_type":"image/png","data":"<base64>"}} (media_type also image/jpeg, image/gif, image/webp); or url — source:{type:'url', url:'...'}. All current models (Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5) support vision and text+image input. Opus 4.7+ added high-resolution vision (up to 2576px long edge, pixel-accurate coordinates) which matters if WorkCrew sends screenshots for the agent to reason over.

PROMPT CACHING (major cost lever for WorkCrew's fixed system prompt + tool list): caching is a PREFIX match in render order tools -> system -> messages; any byte change invalidates everything after it. Add cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'} (5-min TTL; or {type:'ephemeral', ttl:'1h'}) on the last stable content block, or set top-level cache_control on messages.create() to auto-cache the last cacheable block. Max 4 breakpoints/request. Minimum cacheable prefix: 4096 tokens on Opus 4.8/Haiku 4.5, 2048 on Sonnet 4.6 (shorter prefixes silently do not cache). Economics: cache reads ~0.1x input price; writes 1.25x (5-min) / 2x (1-hour). Verify via usage.cache_read_input_tokens > 0 across repeated requests. Silent invalidators to avoid: Date.now()/timestamps or UUIDs in the system prompt, non-deterministic JSON key order, changing the tool set or model mid-conversation. Keep WorkCrew's system prompt and tool list frozen and inject per-request context as later messages.

CURRENT MODEL IDS + PRICING (verified live, June 2026): Use the EXACT id strings, no date suffix on aliases. Claude Opus 4.8 — id claude-opus-4-8, 1M context, 128K max output, $5 input / $25 output per MTok, adaptive thinking, vision, structured outputs. Default for launch-quality WorkCrew. Claude Sonnet 4.6 — claude-sonnet-4-6, 1M context, 64K max output, $3 / $15, adaptive thinking. Best speed/intelligence balance for high-volume chat. Claude Haiku 4.5 — claude-haiku-4-5 (full id claude-haiku-4-5-20251001), 200K context, 64K max output, $1 / $5, fastest/cheapest, NO adaptive thinking. There is no 'claude-haiku-4-x' beyond 4.5 and no Opus 4.x newer than 4.8 at this date. Highest-capability tier is Claude Fable 5 (claude-fable-5, $10/$50, 1M context, always-on thinking) — only if WorkCrew explicitly needs the ceiling; it has extra rules (refusal stop_reason handling, 30-day data retention required, no prefill).

MAX_TOKENS guidance: max_tokens is REQUIRED and is an enforced ceiling on OUTPUT tokens only (the model is not aware of it). Recommended defaults: ~16000 for non-streaming requests (keeps under HTTP timeouts), ~64000 for streaming. Lower deliberately for classification (~256) or cost caps. Opus 4.8 supports up to 128K output but the SDK requires streaming for values that large. If stop_reason === 'max_tokens', output was truncated — raise the cap or switch to streaming + finalMessage().

TOKEN COUNTING: use POST /v1/messages/count_tokens (TS: await client.messages.countTokens({model, system, messages, tools}) -> .input_tokens) for accurate, model-specific counts. Do NOT use tiktoken — it is OpenAI's tokenizer and undercounts Claude by ~15-20% (more on code/non-English). Count_tokens is stateless; pass the same model id you will use for inference. Useful in WorkCrew to estimate cost before sending and to detect when a conversation needs trimming/compaction.

ADAPTIVE THINKING (Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 4.6): for non-trivial agent reasoning, set thinking:{type:'adaptive'} (Claude decides depth; no budget_tokens). budget_tokens is REMOVED on Opus 4.8 (sending it 400s); same for temperature/top_p/top_k (removed/rejected on Opus 4.8). Control depth with output_config:{effort: 'low'|'medium'|'high'|'xhigh'|'max'} (default high). thinking.display defaults to 'omitted' (empty thinking text) on Opus 4.8 — if WorkCrew shows a 'thinking...' indicator with content, set thinking:{type:'adaptive', display:'summarized'}. Echo thinking blocks back unchanged on subsequent same-model turns.

LONG CONVERSATIONS: for chats that may exceed context, enable server-side compaction (beta header compact-2026-01-12, context_management:{edits:[{type:'compact_20260112'}]}) — but you MUST append the full response.content (including the compaction block) back to messages each turn, not just the text, or compaction state is lost. Alternatively cap history client-side using count_tokens.
recommendations: KEY STAYS SERVER-SIDE: Initialize the SDK only in the Fastify backend with `const client = new Anthropic()` reading ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env (never hardcode, never ship to the renderer). The Electron renderer calls WorkCrew backend routes (e.g. POST /api/chat), the backend calls Anthropic, and streams results back. This is non-negotiable for a distributed desktop app — a bundled key is extractable from the Electron app.

RECOMMENDED CHAT ENDPOINT PATTERN (Fastify + TS): create a route that (1) loads the conversation's messages[] from libSQL, (2) appends the new user turn, (3) opens client.messages.stream({model, max_tokens:64000, system:[{type:'text', text: SYSTEM_PROMPT, cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'}}], tools, messages}), (4) sets reply headers for SSE (Content-Type: text/event-stream, Cache-Control: no-cache, Connection: keep-alive) and writes each text_delta to the response as it arrives, (5) calls stream.finalMessage() to get the complete assistant Message, (6) persists response.content back into messages[]. The renderer consumes the SSE stream and appends deltas live.

AGENT ACTIONS AS TOOLS: model each WorkCrew capability (pywinauto desktop actions, Playwright browser actions) as a dedicated custom tool with a typed input_schema, defined in your shared Zod contracts package and converted to JSON Schema for the tools[] array. Use the MANUAL agentic loop (not the auto tool runner) so the backend can require user confirmation before executing destructive/irreversible actions (sending messages, deleting, money movement) — gate on tool name before dispatching to the pywinauto helper or Playwright. Return tool_result with is_error:true on failures so Claude can adapt.

USE THE OFFICIAL SDK EVERYWHERE: install @anthropic-ai/sdk; use Anthropic.MessageParam / Anthropic.Tool / Anthropic.Message / Anthropic.ToolUseBlock types in the backend and shared contracts rather than redefining interfaces. Use stream.finalMessage() to collect the full message instead of hand-wiring .on() promises. Use SDK typed exceptions (Anthropic.RateLimitError, Anthropic.APIError, Anthropic.AuthenticationError) — the SDK auto-retries 429/5xx with backoff (max_retries default 2).

COST/PERF DEFAULTS FOR LAUNCH: default chat model claude-opus-4-8 for quality; expose a model switch to claude-sonnet-4-6 for cost-sensitive/high-volume use and claude-haiku-4-5 for cheap quick tasks. Put the frozen system prompt + tool definitions behind a cache_control breakpoint to get ~90% input savings on the repeated prefix (verify with usage.cache_read_input_tokens). Use count_tokens to show users/admins token estimates and to trim or compact history before hitting the context window.

PDF/IMAGE UX: for user-attached PDFs, send them as document blocks placed before the text prompt; for files reused across turns, upload once via POST /v1/files (anthropic-beta: files-api-2025-04-14) and reference by file_id to keep request payloads under the 32 MB limit. For screenshots the agent must reason over, send image blocks (base64 png/jpeg). Respect the 600-page (100 on Haiku) and 32 MB request limits — chunk large PDFs.

VERIFY MODEL FACTS AT BUILD TIME: query GET /v1/models/{id} (or client.models.retrieve) to confirm context window, max output, and capability flags rather than hardcoding, since model ids/limits change; treat the cached pricing table as a snapshot. Add a startup assertion that response.model.startsWith the configured id after the first call.
risks: API key leakage is the top risk: if any Claude call is made from the Electron renderer (or the key is bundled into the app), it is trivially extractable. All Anthropic traffic must originate from the Fastify backend.
Non-streaming requests with high max_tokens (>~16K) hit SDK/HTTP timeouts; chat and agent turns must stream. Opus 4.8 128K output REQUIRES streaming.

Forgetting to append the full response.content (tool_use and, on thinking models, thinking blocks) back into messages[] breaks the tool loop and multi-turn coherence. Append the whole content array, not the text string.
Each tool_result must carry the exact matching tool_use_id; mismatches or missing results 400. Send all results for a multi-tool response in a single user message.

Prompt-cache silent invalidation: a timestamp/UUID in the system prompt, non-deterministic JSON key order, or changing the tool set/model mid-conversation silently kills caching (cache_read_input_tokens stays 0) and quietly inflates cost. Keep the prefix byte-stable.

Model-id drift and breaking params: do not append date suffixes to aliases (404), and do not send budget_tokens / temperature / top_p / top_k on Opus 4.8 (400). On Opus 4.8 thinking.display defaults to omitted, so a naive 'show thinking' UI will render empty unless display:'summarized' is set.
Refusal handling: stop_reason can be 'refusal' (HTTP 200) — code that reads response.content[0].text unconditionally will throw on refused requests. Branch on stop_reason first.
Request size limits: 32 MB total payload and 600/100 page caps for PDFs; large base64 attachments can blow past these — prefer the Files API and chunk big documents.

Auto-executing destructive desktop/browser tools without confirmation is dangerous in an agent app; use the manual loop with a confirmation gate rather than the auto tool runner for side-effecting WorkCrew actions.

area: Driving a user's existing Chrome (saved logins/cookies) from a Node/Electron app with Playwright in headed mode
summary: For a non-technical user whose Chrome is already open and logged in, the single most reliable approach on Windows is chromium.connectOverCDP attaching to the user's real Chrome started with --remote-debugging-port. The fundamental Windows constraint is that a Chrome user-data-dir can only be owned by ONE chrome.exe process at a time (enforced by Chrome's ProcessSingleton / SingletonLock). Because of this, launchPersistentContext against the user's live profile fails the moment their Chrome is already running: Playwright spawns chrome.exe, it prints "Opening in existing browser session" and immediately exits with code 0, so no context is returned. connectOverCDP avoids the lock entirely because it attaches to the already-running process rather than spawning a second one, and it inherits all cookies/logins/extensions live. The @playwright/cli error "too many arguments. Expected 1 argument but got 2" is a Commander.js positional-argument-count error: the daemon's `open` command accepts exactly ONE positional (the URL); passing the URL plus a second bare token (commonly a value that was meant to be a flag, or `--headed` written in a position the parser treated as positional, or two URLs) trips allowExcessArguments(false). The fix is to pass exactly one positional URL and supply everything else as `--flags` (e.g. `playwright-cli open https://example.com --headed`), or use `attach --cdp=chrome` to reach the existing browser.
findings: APPROACH (a) launchPersistentContext with the user's REAL Chrome user-data-dir + channel:'chrome'. Signature: chromium.launchPersistentContext(userDataDir, { channel: 'chrome', headless: false }). This launches a brand-new chrome.exe that OWNS the given profile directory. Two hard problems: (1) Profile lock. Chrome enforces single-ownership of a user-data-dir via ProcessSingleton (SingletonLock/SingletonCookie/SingletonSocket files on disk). Playwright's own docs state: 'browsers do not allow launching multiple instances with the same User Data Directory.' If the user already has Chrome open on that profile, the new process detects the existing instance, hands off the URL, prints '[out] Opening in existing browser session', and exits (exitCode=0) so Playwright never gets a usable context. (2) Official Playwright guidance explicitly warns AGAINST automating the default Chrome profile: 'Automating the default Chrome user profile is not supported. Pointing userDataDir to Chrome's main User Data directory ... may result in pages not loading or the browser exiting.' There are also reports of profile-path rewriting (Profile 3 -> Profile 3/Default) and profile DB corruption / leftover SingletonLock after unclean shutdown (microsoft/playwright #35466, #5258, #35836).

APPROACH (a) only works reliably if you make a COPY of the user-data-dir and point Playwright at the copy (so it never contends with the live Chrome). The copy carries over cookies/logins at copy-time but goes stale afterward and doubles disk usage; it also will not reflect logins the user creates later. So persistent-context-on-a-copy is good for 'isolated automation that started from the user's session', NOT for 'drive the exact Chrome the user is looking at right now'.

APPROACH (b) connectOverCDP to Chrome started with --remote-debugging-port=PORT. Start (or have the user's Chrome already started) with `chrome.exe --remote-debugging-port=9222`, then read http://localhost:9222/json/version to get webSocketDebuggerUrl, then chromium.connectOverCDP('http://localhost:9222'). Playwright attaches to the LIVE process, so it reuses the user's real cookies, logged-in sessions, and installed extensions with zero copying. Crucially this does NOT trigger the profile lock because no second chrome.exe is spawned. browser.contexts()[0] gives the existing default context and its open tabs/pages. This is the documented way to 'reuse active tabs, cookies, or logged-in sessions'. Headed visibility is inherent because you are attaching to the window the user already sees. The catch: a normally-launched Chrome does NOT expose the debug port. You must (1) relaunch Chrome with the flag, or (2) on recent Chrome, enable it via chrome://inspect/#remote-debugging 'Allow remote debugging for this browser instance'. Also note Chrome since ~v136 restricts --remote-debugging-port on the DEFAULT user-data-dir for security; you may need to pass an explicit --user-data-dir matching the user's profile or use a copied profile dir.

APPROACH (c) @playwright/cli (the agent-cli / 'Playwright CLI'). It runs a long-lived DAEMON (persistent browser process, no per-command startup cost). Commands: `open [url]` (start/navigate), plus click/fill/type/press/screenshot/eval, goto/go-back/reload, tab-*, state-save/state-load, cookie-set, route-*, sessions via `-s=<name>`, `list`, `close-all`. The `open` command flags include --headed, --browser=<chrome|firefox|webkit|msedge>, --channel, --persistent, --profile=<path>, --config=<file.json>. To attach to an EXISTING browser there is a separate `attach` command: `playwright-cli attach --cdp=chrome` (by channel), `attach --cdp=<ws-or-http-url>` (CDP endpoint), `attach --endpoint=<url>` (PW server), or `attach --extension[=<channel>]` (via the Playwright browser extension). For --cdp=chrome the target browser must have remote debugging enabled (chrome://inspect/#remote-debugging). So @playwright/cli is essentially a friendlier wrapper over approaches (a) open/--persistent/--profile and (b) attach --cdp.

DIAGNOSIS of 'Daemon process exited with code 1 ... error: too many arguments. Expected 1 argument but got 2.' This is a Commander.js (tj/commander) argument-count error, not a browser error. Commander 7+ added allowExcessArguments(false) which, when a command declares a fixed-arity positional (here `open <url>` expects exactly 1), throws 'error: too many arguments. Expected N argument(s) but got M' if more positionals arrive. 'got 2' means the parser saw TWO positional tokens where the daemon's open command accepts ONE (the URL). Common real causes: (i) a flag value got separated and read as a positional, e.g. the caller built args like ['open', url, 'headed'] or ['open', url, '--headed', 'true'] where 'true' became a 2nd positional; (ii) two URLs / an extra trailing token were passed; (iii) the URL contained an unquoted space or '&' so the shell split it into two args; (iv) '--headed' was placed BEFORE a subcommand or otherwise parsed as a positional. The daemon spawns the CLI, Commander rejects, process exits 1, and the parent reports 'Daemon process exited with code 1'.

FIX for the CLI error: pass exactly ONE positional (the URL) and express everything else as proper --flags, fully quoted. Correct: `playwright-cli open "https://example.com" --headed` (and add `--channel=chrome` or `--browser=chrome` to use real Chrome). When invoking from Node via child_process, use the ARRAY form so nothing is re-split by a shell: spawn(cli, ['open', url, '--headed', '--channel=chrome']) and never concatenate the URL into a string. --headed is a boolean flag and must NOT be given a value. If the intent is to drive the user's already-open Chrome, do not use `open` at all (it launches a browser); use `playwright-cli attach --cdp=chrome` (after enabling remote debugging), which takes no positional URL.

SECURITY: every one of these approaches grants the automation FULL authenticated control of whatever the user is logged into (email, bank, social, work SSO). connectOverCDP and persistent-context-on-the-real-profile are the most powerful and the most dangerous: code driving that context can read mail, send messages, change settings, or exfiltrate session cookies, all as the real user with no extra login prompt. A debug port (--remote-debugging-port) is also a local attack surface: any local process can connect to 127.0.0.1:9222 and hijack the session, so it must be bound to localhost only, ideally on an ephemeral random port, opened only while needed and closed after. Treat the WorkCrew automation like a privileged actor: scope actions, show the user what it is about to do on a logged-in account, require explicit per-account consent, and prefer a dedicated automation profile over the user's primary identity when the workflow allows.
recommendations: RECOMMENDED for a non-technical user whose Chrome is already open and logged in: use connectOverCDP, but manage the Chrome process yourself rather than relying on the user's manually-launched Chrome. Concretely: (1) Detect the user's profile path (chrome://version Profile Path, or %LOCALAPPDATA%/Google/Chrome/User Data + the right Profile folder). (2) Launch (or relaunch) Chrome yourself with `--remote-debugging-port=<random ephemeral port>` and `--user-data-dir=<their profile>` (or a copy, see profile-lock handling). (3) connectOverCDP('http://127.0.0.1:<port>'), grab browser.contexts()[0], and drive the existing pages. This gives live logins, true headed visibility (it is literally their window), and no separate login step.

Handle the profile lock GRACEFULLY instead of erroring. Flow: (a) Probe whether a CDP endpoint already exists (GET http://127.0.0.1:<port>/json/version). If yes, just connectOverCDP and skip launching. (b) If not, check whether Chrome is already running on that profile (presence of SingletonLock in the user-data-dir, or a running chrome.exe owning it). If it is running WITHOUT the debug port, you cannot attach. Present a single friendly choice: 'WorkCrew needs to connect to your Chrome. Click Restart Chrome to continue (your tabs will reopen).' Then close Chrome cleanly and relaunch it yourself with the debug port. (c) NEVER try launchPersistentContext against the live profile while their Chrome is open. (d) As a fallback that needs no restart, offer to drive a COPIED profile (copy user-data-dir to an app-owned dir, launch persistent context on the copy) so the user keeps browsing while WorkCrew works on a snapshot of their session.

Do NOT make launchPersistentContext-on-the-default-profile your primary path: it is explicitly unsupported by Playwright, it fights the profile lock, and it can corrupt or rewrite the user's profile (#35466, #5258, #35836). Reserve persistent context for the copied-profile fallback only.

Prefer connectOverCDP over @playwright/cli for the embedded Electron use case. The CLI/daemon adds a child-process + Commander argument-parsing layer (the exact source of the 'too many arguments' failure you hit) and a second moving part to ship and debug. The Playwright library gives you direct, typed control of the same CDP attach with better error handling. If you do keep the CLI, always invoke it with spawn(arg-array), one positional URL, quoted, and use `attach --cdp=chrome` for the existing-browser case rather than `open`.

Security hardening for launch: bind the debug port to 127.0.0.1, use a fresh random port per session (not a fixed 9222), open it only for the duration of the automation and tear it down after, and surface to the user (in WorkCrew's UI) which logged-in account an action will run against before executing anything sensitive. Where the workflow does not strictly need the user's primary identity, run on a dedicated automation Chrome profile to limit blast radius.

Be aware of Chrome's recent security change (Chrome ~136+) that blocks --remote-debugging-port when pointed at the DEFAULT user-data-dir. Mitigate by always passing an explicit --user-data-dir (the user's real profile path or a copy) when you launch with the debug flag, and test against current stable Chrome before launch.
risks: Profile lock: any approach that spawns a second chrome.exe on a user-data-dir already owned by the user's open Chrome will fail silently (Chrome prints 'Opening in existing browser session' and exits 0; Playwright gets no context). This is the #1 cause of 'it works when Chrome is closed but not when it's open'.

Profile corruption / unexpected modification when using launchPersistentContext on the real profile (leftover SingletonLock after unclean exit, profile-path rewrite, cookie-DB issues). Can degrade or break the user's normal Chrome.
Chrome 136+ restricts remote debugging on the default profile, so a naive --remote-debugging-port without an explicit --user-data-dir may not expose the port at all.

Security: live authenticated control of all the user's logged-in accounts; an open localhost debug port is hijackable by any local process. Must be localhost-only, ephemeral, time-bounded, and consent-gated.

@playwright/cli adds a daemon + Commander.js arg-parsing surface; mis-shaped args (extra positional, unquoted URL with spaces/&, --headed given a value) produce 'too many arguments' / 'Daemon exited code 1' that look like Playwright bugs but are CLI usage errors.
Copied-profile fallback drifts from the live session over time and duplicates disk usage; logins created after the copy will be missing.

area: Robust real-world pywinauto automation on Windows 11 for WorkCrew local Windows automation
summary: pywinauto exposes two backends: win32 (default, fast, for legacy MFC/VB6/VCL/WinForms/old apps) and uia (Microsoft UI Automation, for WPF/WinForms/Store apps/Qt5/browsers and modern controls). The decision rule is tooling-driven: if Spy++ shows all controls use win32; if Inspect.exe (UIA mode) shows more controls than Spy++ use uia. Reliable automation rests on three pillars: (1) identify controls by stable, multi-criteria specs (auto_id + control_type, falling back to title/best_match), never by index alone; (2) always wait for state (window.wait('ready'), wait_until_passes) rather than sleeping; (3) prefer real input emulation (click_input, type_keys with proper waits) for cross-process and rendering-sensitive UI, and direct API methods (set_text/set_edit_text, invoke) when speed and reliability matter. Hard constraints to design around: an elevated/admin target requires WorkCrew's helper to also run elevated; the Windows UAC consent prompt runs on the Secure Desktop and CANNOT be automated by any user-mode tool; custom canvas controls expose no UIA tree and must be driven by coordinate-based mouse/keyboard; and DPI awareness must be enabled to get correct coordinates. For headed/interactive mode (WorkCrew's case) this is the right fit, since pywinauto needs a real visible desktop session.
findings: BACKEND CHOICE: Set per-Application via Application(backend="uia") or backend="win32" (win32 is default). win32 supports MFC, VB6, VCL, simple WinForms, most legacy apps. uia supports WinForms, WPF, Store/UWP apps, Qt5, and browsers. Decision rule from official docs: if Spy++ shows all controls -> win32; if Inspect.exe in UIA mode shows more controls/properties than Spy++ -> uia. Key gap: the UIA API may not provide a window handle for every element, and such handle-less elements are invisible to the win32 backend, which is the most common reason a script that 'finds nothing' under win32 works under uia. Source: pywinauto getting_started docs and GitHub README.

CONTROL IDENTIFICATION (most reliable -> least): Use child_window()/window() with explicit criteria combining auto_id and control_type, e.g. dlg.child_window(auto_id='num8Button', control_type='Button') or app.Properties.child_window(title='Contains:', auto_id='13087', control_type='Edit'). auto_id maps to AutomationId (UIA) / the win32 control_id and is the most stable handle. Magic/attribute access (app.UntitledNotepad) is shorthand for window(best_match=...). best_match is a fuzzy matcher and is convenient but order/locale fragile; do not ship code that relies on Button3-style numeric best_match names (Button0 and Button1 can reference the same control). Use print_control_identifiers() during development to discover auto_id/title/control_type and the suggested child_window() spec. For production set allow_magic_lookup=False on Application/Desktop to force explicit specs and fail fast instead of deferring errors.

WAITS AND TIMING (never use bare sleep): A control spec is lazy -- it is resolved only when an action is called or wrapper_object() is invoked (raises ElementNotFoundError if missing). Use dlg.wait('exists enabled visible ready', timeout=..., retry_interval=...) and dlg.wait_not('visible', ...) to gate on state. For arbitrary conditions use pywinauto.timings.wait_until(timeout, retry_interval, func, value=True) and wait_until_passes(timeout, retry_interval, func, exceptions) which retries until func stops raising. Global tuning via pywinauto.timings.Timings: defaults include window_find_timeout=5s, window_find_retry=0.09s, app_start_timeout=10s, app_connect_timeout=5s, after_click_wait=0.09s, after_sendkeys_key_wait=0.01s, setfocus_timeout=2s, after_windowclose_timeout=2s. Timings.slow() multiplies timeouts x10 (good for flaky/slow machines or first-run cold starts), Timings.fast() collapses waits to near-zero (risky), Timings.defaults() restores. WorkCrew should bump these on slower client machines rather than sprinkling time.sleep.

TYPING TEXT SAFELY: Two distinct mechanisms. (a) set_text()/set_edit_text() writes directly to the control via API -- fast, atomic, no focus stealing, ideal for Edit fields and form filling; preferred when it works. (b) type_keys('...') emulates real keystrokes and is required for controls that only respond to real input or apps that validate on keypress. type_keys interprets special syntax in braces ({ENTER}, {TAB}, {HOME}, {DOWN 2}); literal text with spaces REQUIRES with_spaces=True (without it spaces are dropped), and use with_tabs/with_newlines similarly. Escape special chars or use pause/set with after_sendkeys_key_wait for apps that drop fast keystrokes. For pasting large/unicode text reliably, set_text or clipboard paste is safer than type_keys.

CLICKING: click() sends a programmatic click message (fast, works on background/unfocused windows, but some custom controls ignore synthetic messages); click_input() physically moves the mouse and clicks at real screen coordinates (slower, requires the window to be visible/foregrounded but works on virtually everything including custom-drawn controls). Rule for WorkCrew headed mode: prefer click()/invoke() for standard controls; fall back to click_input() (after set_focus()) for custom or canvas controls. Same split exists for typing: type_keys (messages) vs send keys to focused window.

SCREENSHOTS: ctrl.capture_as_image() returns a PIL Image of a specific window/control which you can .save('out.png'). This captures the actual window region (respects what is rendered) and is the correct primitive for WorkCrew to attach evidence/verification screenshots per action. Requires Pillow installed. Note it captures the on-screen pixels, so the window must be visible and not occluded for a meaningful shot.

STARTING vs CONNECTING: Application().start(r'path\app.exe', timeout=...) launches and retries until the main window appears (uses app_start_timeout/retry). Application().connect(process=pid | handle=hwnd | path='app.exe' | title_re=...) attaches to a running process and does NOT auto-retry, so wrap connect in wait_until_passes for race-free attach. Use the Desktop(backend='uia') object for windows that cross process boundaries (dialogs, taskbar, Start) -- e.g. Desktop(backend='uia').Calculator because modern Calculator and File Explorer draw across multiple processes and Application (process-scoped) will miss child dialogs.

COMMON APPS: Notepad -> works under win32; classic edit control, use set_edit_text or type_keys, menu_select('File->SaveAs') or for Win11 Notepad use uia. Calculator (Win10/11 UWP) -> must use uia and the Desktop object (Desktop(backend='uia').window(title='Calculator')) because it is a Store app drawing in multiple processes; press buttons by auto_id like 'num8Button','plusButton','equalButton'. File Explorer -> connect to explorer.exe via uia/Desktop by title; the file list is an ItemsView/list control, set_focus then iterate items, right_click_input for context menus, and handle the resulting dialogs via Desktop since they are separate top-level windows. Office (Word/Excel/Outlook) -> uia exposes the ribbon and document panes but they are large/slow trees; prefer the application COM automation (win32com.client) for document content and reserve pywinauto for UI-only actions (dialogs, ribbon buttons) it cannot reach via COM.

HARD LIMITATIONS (design constraints): (1) ELEVATION: to automate an app running as Administrator, the pywinauto process itself MUST run elevated; a non-elevated process cannot see or send input to an elevated window (UIPI). You cannot elevate part of a script -- the whole process must be relaunched elevated (e.g. via a UAC re-spawn helper like pyuac/ShellExecute runas). (2) SECURE DESKTOP / UAC PROMPT: the UAC consent dialog renders on the isolated Secure Desktop and is NOT automatable by ANY user-mode tool including pywinauto -- WorkCrew must surface these to the human or pre-elevate to avoid them; likewise the Windows lock screen, login screen, and Ctrl+Alt+Del screen. (3) CUSTOM/CANVAS CONTROLS: apps that draw their own UI on a single canvas (some games, Electron/Chromium content without accessibility flags, custom GDI controls) expose no/empty UIA tree -- pywinauto cannot find sub-controls and you must drive them with coordinate-based mouse/keyboard (pywinauto.mouse, pywinauto.keyboard) plus image/OCR matching. Chromium/Chrome needs --force-renderer-accessibility for its content to appear in UIA. (4) DPI SCALING: enable per-monitor DPI awareness in the process (pywinauto added DPI-awareness API support for Win8+); without it coordinates and click_input targets are wrong on 125%/150%/200% displays and mixed-DPI multi-monitor setups.

CONCURRENCY/SESSION: pywinauto drives real input on the interactive desktop, so (a) only one automation should manipulate the desktop at a time -- treat it as a single shared resource and serialize WorkCrew actions; (b) it requires an unlocked, visible interactive session (headed mode) -- it does not work on a locked workstation or pure headless service with no desktop; (c) click_input/type_keys steal the foreground and real cursor, so the machine is effectively in-use during automation. Background message-based click()/set_text() avoid stealing focus and are preferable when reliable.

SAFETY / ROBUSTNESS PATTERNS for WorkCrew: (1) Always wrap a logical step in wait_until_passes/window.wait so a missing window raises a typed error you can catch and retry rather than acting on the wrong window. (2) Verify identity before acting: call draw_outline() in debug builds and assert window_text()/control_type before destructive actions. (3) Confirm before acting -- read current control state (get_value/texts/is_checked) and only act if it differs, making actions idempotent and re-runnable. (4) Set a per-action timeout budget and a global Timings profile chosen per machine speed. (5) Prefer API actions (set_text, invoke, click) over coordinate clicks; only drop to coordinate mouse for canvas controls, and recompute coordinates from the live rectangle() each time (never hard-code pixels). (6) Capture capture_as_image() before/after each action for an audit trail. (7) Run the helper elevated only when the target is elevated, and otherwise least-privilege; detect elevation mismatch (ElementNotFoundError on a clearly-present window often means an elevation gap) and surface a clear message. (8) Guard against the Secure Desktop: never assume a UAC prompt will be clickable; pre-authorize or ask the user.
recommendations: Make backend a per-target setting in WorkCrew with a sensible default of uia for modern Windows 11 apps and an explicit win32 opt-in for legacy/classic apps; expose a developer command that runs print_control_identifiers() so authors can capture auto_id/control_type/best_match for each automation recipe.

Standardize control specs on child_window(auto_id=..., control_type=...) with title/best_match only as fallback, and set allow_magic_lookup=False in production code paths so missing controls fail fast with ElementNotFoundError instead of silently mismatching.

Build a thin wrapper layer that (a) always calls spec.wait('exists visible enabled ready', timeout) before acting, (b) chooses set_text/invoke/click for standard controls and click_input/type_keys(with_spaces=True) for canvas/custom controls, and (c) takes capture_as_image() before and after each action for the audit log.

Expose a single Timings profile knob (fast/default/slow) selectable per client machine; default to a slightly slowed profile on first run / cold start, and never use raw time.sleep -- use wait_until/wait_until_passes for conditions.

Enable per-monitor-v2 DPI awareness in the Python helper process at startup (manifest or SetProcessDpiAwarenessContext) so click_input coordinates are correct on scaled and multi-monitor Windows 11 setups, and always derive coordinates from the live control rectangle() at action time.

Detect and handle elevation explicitly: ship the pywinauto helper able to relaunch itself elevated (UAC runas) only when the target window is elevated; treat ElementNotFoundError on a visibly-present elevated window as an elevation mismatch and report it to the user rather than retrying forever.

Never attempt to automate the UAC consent prompt, lock/login screens, or Ctrl+Alt+Del; instead pre-elevate or pause and request human confirmation. Document this as a hard boundary in WorkCrew's safety model.

For Chromium/Electron content targets, require launching with --force-renderer-accessibility (or use the Playwright path WorkCrew already has for browsers) instead of pywinauto; for Office content prefer COM (win32com) and use pywinauto only for dialogs/ribbon the COM API cannot reach.

Serialize all desktop-input automation through a single queue/lock since the interactive desktop is a shared singleton resource, and gate execution on an unlocked, visible session (headed mode); fail clearly if the session is locked.
Add an idempotency check to every action recipe: read current state (get_value/texts/is_checked) and skip the action if already in the desired state, so retries and re-runs are safe.
risks: Elevation mismatch is the most common silent failure: a non-elevated WorkCrew helper will simply not see or control elevated apps, surfacing as confusing ElementNotFoundError even though the window is plainly visible. Must be detected and messaged.
UAC consent prompts and other Secure Desktop screens are fundamentally non-automatable; any recipe that triggers one will stall unless WorkCrew pre-elevates or hands off to the user.

Coordinate-based fallbacks for canvas/custom controls are brittle across DPI, theme, window-size, and resolution changes; hard-coded pixels will break. Always recompute from live rectangle() and prefer accessibility/API paths.
best_match and attribute (magic) lookup are locale- and order-sensitive and can match the wrong control as apps change; relying on them in shipped recipes causes flaky, hard-to-debug failures.

click_input/type_keys steal the real mouse and keyboard, so automation visibly takes over the user's machine and any concurrent human activity (or a second automation) will corrupt the run; the desktop must be treated as exclusive.
pywinauto requires a visible, unlocked interactive session -- it will not work as a true background/headless service, which constrains how WorkCrew can schedule unattended runs.
UIA trees for heavy apps (Office, large WPF) can be slow to enumerate, causing timeouts; over-broad searches (e.g. descendants without control_type) can hang or pick wrong elements.
Version drift on Windows 11 (Notepad rewrite, evolving File Explorer, new Calculator) changes auto_ids and tree structure between OS builds, so recipes need maintenance and defensive fallbacks.

area: Local-first authentication and hard paywall for WorkCrew, designed for clean later swap to Supabase Auth + Stripe Billing
summary: Build auth and entitlement as a backend-only concern in the Fastify server with libSQL/SQLite, never in the Electron renderer. Store passwords with argon2id (node-argon2, the `argon2` npm package, which defaults to argon2id) using OWASP-aligned parameters, add an HMAC pepper via the library's `secret` option, and issue a short-lived signed access token plus a single-use rotating refresh token. Model entitlements as a separate table whose `status` field uses Stripe's exact vocabulary (active, trialing, past_due, canceled, incomplete, incomplete_expired, unpaid) so a real Stripe webhook can later write the same rows with zero schema change. Enforce a HARD paywall server-side: every protected route (chat, model calls, automation) runs a single `requireActiveEntitlement` guard that re-reads the DB and rejects unless status is active/trialing and the period has not expired. The client only ever receives allow/deny; it never decides access. Keep providers behind two interfaces (AuthProvider, BillingProvider) with a LocalAuthProvider/SimulatedBillingProvider today and SupabaseAuthProvider/StripeBillingProvider later, selected by a config flag so the swap is a config and adapter change, not a rewrite.
findings: PASSWORD HASHING: OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet recommends argon2id with one of these equivalent configs (all acceptable): m=47104 KiB (46 MiB)/t=1/p=1, OR m=19456 KiB (19 MiB)/t=2/p=1, OR m=12288 (12 MiB)/t=3/p=1. The hard minimum is 19 MiB memory, 2 iterations, 1 parallelism. The whole hash should complete in under ~1 second on your target hardware; calibrate on a typical user laptop.

ARGON2 LIBRARY: The `argon2` npm package (node-argon2 by ranisalt) defaults to type=argon2id, hashLength=32, timeCost=3, memoryCost=65536 KiB (64 MiB), parallelism=4, with an auto-generated cryptographically random salt embedded in the PHC-format output string. API is `await argon2.hash(password, opts)` and `await argon2.verify(storedHash, password)`. The library's defaults already exceed OWASP minimums; for a single-process desktop backend you may lower parallelism to 1 and memory to ~19-47 MiB to keep p in line with a 1-thread server. The PHC string self-describes its params, so verify() needs no stored config and supports painless param upgrades later.

PEPPER: Pass a server-held secret as the `secret` Buffer option to argon2.hash/verify. This is a pepper (extra keyed input not stored in the DB), giving defense-in-depth if the SQLite file leaks. On desktop, store the pepper outside the DB file (OS keychain via keytar / Electron safeStorage, or an env/config value), NOT hardcoded. OWASP notes a pepper can also be applied as an outer HMAC; the library `secret` option is the cleaner built-in path.

SCRYPT FALLBACK: If a native argon2 build is unavailable on a target Windows machine, OWASP fallback is scrypt with N=2^17 (128 MiB)/r=8/p=1 (minimum), or lighter equivalents like N=2^16/r=8/p=2. Node has built-in `crypto.scrypt`, so this is a zero-native-dependency fallback. Record the algorithm tag in the stored hash so both can coexist.

SESSIONS/TOKENS: Mirror Supabase's model so swapping is seamless. Supabase defaults: access token = signed JWT, default lifetime 1 hour (do not go below 5 min); refresh token = single-use opaque random string that ROTATES on each exchange, with a ~10-second reuse grace window for races (SSR/retries). Reuse of a refresh token outside that window invalidates the ENTIRE session (reuse detection / theft response). Sessions live in an auth.sessions table keyed by a session_id (UUID) that is also a JWT claim; sessions end on sign-out, password change, inactivity timeout, max-lifetime time-box, or sign-in-elsewhere. Expired sessions are garbage-collected ~24h after expiry.

LOCAL TOKEN DESIGN: For the local stub, sign access JWTs with a local server secret (HS256 via jose/jsonwebtoken), 15-60 min expiry, claims sub (userId), sessionId, entitlementStatus snapshot (advisory only, never trusted for gating), iat, exp. Store refresh tokens HASHED (sha256) in a refresh_tokens table, never plaintext, with single-use rotation: on refresh, mark old token used, issue new pair, and if a used/unknown token is presented revoke the whole session. This is byte-for-byte the behavior SupabaseAuthProvider will later replace.

STRIPE STATUS VOCABULARY (use verbatim as your entitlement status enum): incomplete (created, first invoice unpaid), incomplete_expired (first invoice unpaid within ~23h, terminal), trialing (in trial, becomes active when trial ends), active (paid/current), past_due (latest renewal invoice failed), canceled (ended), unpaid (renewal unpaid, invoices left open). Treating these as your native status values means a future Stripe webhook handler writes the same strings with no translation layer.

STRIPE PROVISIONING FLOW TO MIMIC: Real Stripe provisions via webhooks: checkout.session.completed (mode === 'subscription') grants initial access; customer.subscription.created/updated/deleted and invoice.paid/invoice.payment_failed keep status and current_period_end in sync. Best practices to replicate now so the local path is identical: (1) verify webhook signature, (2) process idempotently by storing handled event IDs and skipping duplicates (Stripe retries), (3) persist status, current_period_end, cancel_at_period_end, customerId, subscriptionId. Your SimulatedBillingProvider should emit the same internal event shape into the same handler.

HARD PAYWALL GATE: The gate is server-side and unconditional. A single guard requireActiveEntitlement(userId) reads the entitlement row fresh from the DB on EVERY protected request and allows only if status in {active, trialing} AND current_period_end > now() (with a small grace, e.g. honor past_due for a short configurable window only if you choose a soft-dunning policy; default = deny). All model/chat/automation routes are registered behind this guard (Fastify preHandler hook). The renderer receives only 200 with data or 402/403; it must hold no key, model endpoint, or 'isPaid' flag that unlocks anything locally. Even the LLM API key lives only in the backend and is used only after the gate passes.

CLEAN BOUNDARY FOR SWAP: Define two TypeScript interfaces in the shared contracts package: AuthProvider { signUp, signIn, verifyAccessToken, refresh, signOut, getUser } and BillingProvider { startCheckout, getEntitlement, handleWebhook }. Today wire LocalAuthProvider (argon2 + local JWT) and SimulatedBillingProvider (fake checkout that writes a Stripe-shaped entitlement row). Later wire SupabaseAuthProvider and StripeBillingProvider. Select via a single config flag (AUTH_MODE=local|supabase, BILLING_MODE=simulated|stripe). The Zod entitlement schema and DB columns already use Stripe's field names (status, current_period_end, cancel_at_period_end, customer_id, subscription_id), so only the adapter implementations change.
recommendations: Use the `argon2` npm package (argon2id).
