Config for a single-process backend: { type: argon2.argon2id, memoryCost: 47104, timeCost: 1, parallelism: 1, hashLength: 32, secret: pepperBuffer }, or the 19456/t=2 variant; benchmark on a low-end Windows laptop and pick the config that stays under ~500ms. Keep the full PHC string in the users.password_hash column so params upgrade transparently on next login.

Store the argon2 pepper outside the SQLite file: Electron safeStorage or OS keychain (keytar) in production, env var in dev. Never commit it. Rotating it invalidates all passwords, so treat it as a long-lived secret.

Tables: users(id, email UNIQUE, email_verified INTEGER, password_hash, created_at); sessions(id UUID, user_id, created_at, last_seen_at, expires_at, revoked_at); refresh_tokens(id, session_id, token_hash, created_at, used_at, replaced_by); entitlements(user_id PRIMARY KEY, plan, status TEXT, current_period_end INTEGER, cancel_at_period_end INTEGER, customer_id, subscription_id, updated_at); processed_billing_events(event_id PRIMARY KEY, type, received_at). All defined as Zod schemas in the shared contracts package and reused server + client.

Access token: 30-60 min expiry, HS256 signed with a local secret (jose). Refresh token: 256-bit random, stored sha256-hashed, single-use rotation with whole-session revoke on reuse, ~10s grace to match Supabase. This matches Supabase semantics exactly so SupabaseAuthProvider is a drop-in.

Implement requireActiveEntitlement as a Fastify preHandler applied to every protected route group. Logic: load entitlement by userId from token sub; deny (402 Payment Required) unless status in {active, trialing} and current_period_end > now. Default policy: deny on past_due/canceled/incomplete/unpaid. Make the LLM API key reachable only after this guard, never exposed to the renderer.

Build SimulatedBillingProvider.startCheckout to open a local fake checkout screen, then write an entitlement row with status='active', current_period_end = now + 30 days, and dispatch a synthetic 'customer.subscription.created' event through the SAME handler the real Stripe webhook will use. Add dev-only controls to flip status to past_due/canceled to test the gate against every Stripe state.

Provide an email-verification stub: on sign-up set email_verified=0, generate a verification token, and in local mode auto-print/log the link (or a dev button) that sets email_verified=1. Keep the same verify endpoint shape Supabase uses so it becomes a no-op redirect to Supabase later. Decide policy: verification can be required for sign-in but is separate from the paywall gate.

Put AuthProvider and BillingProvider interfaces plus the entitlement/status Zod enum in the shared contracts package; select implementations by AUTH_MODE / BILLING_MODE config flags. Keep all Stripe field names verbatim in DB and contracts so the only migration to production is swapping adapters and pointing webhooks at the real handler.

Make all billing-event handling idempotent now (processed_billing_events table keyed by event_id) and signature-verified in the Stripe adapter, so the local-to-Stripe transition needs no behavioral change to the entitlement-writing code path.
risks: Trusting the client for entitlement: any 'isPaid' flag, model endpoint, or API key shipped to the renderer breaks the hard paywall. The gate must re-read the DB server-side on every request; the JWT entitlement claim is advisory only and can be stale.

SQLite file theft: the DB sits on the user's machine. argon2 + an externally stored pepper limits offline password cracking, but anyone with the file plus a copy of the pepper could attempt it. Keep the pepper out of the DB and out of source.

Native build fragility: node-argon2 is a native addon and can fail to build/load on some Windows setups; ship a prebuilt binary or implement the crypto.scrypt fallback (N=2^17/r=8/p=1) with an algorithm tag in the hash so both verify.

Local JWT secret compromise lets an attacker mint valid access tokens; still, the entitlement re-check means they cannot grant themselves a paid status without writing the entitlements table. Protect the signing secret like the pepper.

Refresh-token rotation race conditions: without the ~10s grace window, legitimate concurrent refreshes (e.g., app + helper process) can trigger false session revocation. Implement the grace window to match Supabase behavior.

past_due handling is a policy decision: a strict hard paywall denies on past_due, but real Stripe dunning keeps users active briefly during retries. Pick one policy now and encode it in the guard so production behavior is identical.

Forgetting idempotency on billing events: Stripe retries webhooks; non-idempotent handlers can double-provision or corrupt status. Build the processed_events guard before going live so the local and Stripe paths are identical.

area: Document upload and chat-with-your-files for the WorkCrew AI desktop app
summary: For WorkCrew's "chat with your files" feature, route each format to the right Messages API representation and never silently truncate. Use native PDF `document` blocks (server-side rasterizes each page to text+image, so charts/tables/scanned-layout are understood), pass images as `image` blocks, and for the formats Claude does not accept as document blocks (.docx, .md, .txt, .csv, .xlsx) extract to plain text on the client and send a `document` block with `source.type:"text"` so citations still work. Default to inlining content (base64/text) for one-shot chats; upload to the Files API (beta `files-api-2025-04-14`) and reference by `file_id` for files reused across turns or too large to inline. Persist conversations and attachment metadata (not blobs) in the existing libSQL store, keeping the original bytes on disk in the app's user-data directory. Enable citations on every document block for verifiable, source-grounded answers. Do redaction on the extracted text client-side before sending. All findings are current as of June 2026 against claude-opus-4-8.
findings: FORMAT ROUTING. The Messages API accepts only three document-capable representations: (1) `document` block with a PDF source (base64 / url / file_id), (2) `document` block with `source.type:"text", media_type:"text/plain"` for plain text, (3) `image` block for jpeg/png/gif/webp. CSV, XLSX, DOCX, MD, TXT are NOT accepted as document blocks and must be converted to plain text client-side, then sent as a text `document` block (or inline text). Source: platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/files (File types and content blocks table; 'Working with other file formats').

NATIVE PDF vs LOCAL TEXT EXTRACTION (the central decision). Native PDF document blocks: server converts each page to an image AND extracts its text, sending both to the model, so Claude understands charts, tables, diagrams, scanned layout, and visual structure. Use native PDF when visual content matters (financial reports, slides exported to PDF, forms, anything with figures) or when you want reliable page-number citations. Local text extraction is appropriate when: the source is already text-native (.txt/.md/.docx/.csv), you want to minimize tokens (a text-only page costs ~1.5k-3k tokens vs a page also rasterized as an image adding up to ~1568-4784 image tokens), or you need to redact before sending. KEY LIMITATION: a scanned PDF with no extractable text layer is NOT citable (image citations are not supported) and text extraction yields nothing, so such PDFs only work via native PDF blocks for Q&A, not citations. Source: pdf-support.md ('How PDF support works'); citations.md ('PDFs that are scans... will not be citable').

PDF LIMITS. Max request size 32 MB (entire payload, varies by platform); max 600 pages per request for 1M-context models, 100 pages for 200k-context models; standard PDFs only (no passwords/encryption). Both limits cover the whole request, including other content. Dense PDFs can exhaust the context window before the page limit, and very large PDFs can fail even via Files API; recommendation is to split into sections. Each page ~1500-3000 text tokens plus image tokens. Source: pdf-support.md ('Check PDF requirements', 'Estimate your costs').

FILES API (beta `files-api-2025-04-14`). Upload once, reference by `file_id` across many requests; avoids re-encoding and shrinks request payloads. Max file size 500 MB, total 500 GB/org. Files persist until explicitly deleted (DELETE /v1/files/{id}); uploads/downloads/list/metadata/delete are free; content is billed as input tokens only when used in a message. IMPORTANT: files YOU upload cannot be downloaded back (only files created by skills/code execution can). Files are scoped to the API-key's workspace. Beta rate limit ~100 req/min. NOT eligible for Zero Data Retention. Available on Claude API (and Claude Platform on AWS, Microsoft Foundry); not on Bedrock/Vertex. SDK: client.beta.files.upload({file: await toFile(stream, undefined, {type})}) returning {id, filename, mime_type, size_bytes}. Source: files.md.

WHEN TO INLINE vs FILES API. Inline (base64 PDF / inline image / inline text) is simplest and fine for one-shot chats and small files. Use Files API + file_id when: the same document is referenced across multiple conversation turns (avoids re-uploading every stateless request), the file is large (keeps each request payload small and under the 32 MB PDF request limit), or you want to dedupe storage across conversations. Both routes accept citations and prompt caching. Source: pdf-support.md ('Option 3: Files API'), files.md.

CITATIONS (recommended default for chat-with-your-files). Set `citations:{enabled:true}` on each document block (currently all-or-none across documents in a request). Response splits into text blocks each carrying a `citations` array. Citation granularity by type: PDF -> page_location (start/end_page_number, 1-indexed, exclusive end); plain text -> char_location (start/end_char_index, 0-indexed, exclusive); custom content (`source.type:"content"` with a list of text blocks) -> content_block_location (block index range, no extra chunking - use this for transcripts, bullet lists, or to make each RAG/extracted chunk individually citable). `cited_text` is returned for convenience and does NOT count toward output tokens (and not toward input tokens when passed back). All active models except Haiku 3 support citations. CRITICAL INCOMPATIBILITY: citations + Structured Outputs (output_config.format) returns 400 - cannot use both. Optional `title` (short) and `context` (longer, for metadata) fields are passed to the model but not citable. Streaming emits `citations_delta`. Source: citations.md.

IMAGES. Formats jpeg/png/gif/webp; max 10 MB base64 on the Claude API direct; max dimensions 8000x8000 px; up to 100 images/request (200k-context models) or 600 (others). Claude tokenizes in 28x28 px patches: cost = ceil(w/28)*ceil(h/28) visual tokens. On claude-opus-4-8 native resolution caps at 2576 px long edge / 4784 tokens (high-res, ~3x older models' 1568 px / 1568 tokens); larger images are auto-downscaled. To control cost, downsample client-side before upload if fidelity is not needed. Image blocks accept base64, url, or Files API file_id sources. Source: vision.md.

ATTACHMENT UX. Anthropic docs describe the claude.ai pattern (drag-drop into chat, an add-file button, paste an image) - the same three entry points WorkCrew should implement (drag-drop, '+' button, clipboard paste). claude.ai caps 20 images per message; the API allows far more. No special API surface for UX; it is purely how the renderer collects files before building content blocks. Source: vision.md ('How to use vision').

DEFAULT MODEL & PARAMS. Use claude-opus-4-8 (1M context, 128K max output). For chat-with-files use streaming (gives progressive answer + citations_delta) and adaptive thinking (thinking:{type:'adaptive'}). Long document analysis with large max_tokens must stream to avoid SDK HTTP timeouts. Source: claude-api skill defaults + streaming.md.

PROMPT CACHING for documents. Put `cache_control:{type:'ephemeral'}` on the top-level document block so a re-queried document bills cached reads (~0.1x) on follow-up turns; citation blocks themselves are not cacheable but the source documents are. Minimum cacheable prefix is 4096 tokens on Opus 4.8. This is the main cost lever for multi-turn chat over the same file. Source: citations.md ('Using Prompt Caching with Citations'), prompt-caching.md.
recommendations: FORMAT->TRANSPORT MAP (implement as a single dispatcher in the backend). PDF -> native `document` block (Files API file_id when reused or >~20MB, else base64), citations on. Images (jpg/png/gif/webp) -> `image` block (downsample to <=2576 px long edge client-side to cap tokens). .txt/.md -> read as UTF-8, send `document` block source.type:'text', citations on. .docx -> extract text client-side (e.g. mammoth) for text-native docs; for DOCX with important images, convert to PDF first and use native PDF (docs explicitly recommend this). .csv/.xlsx -> parse to a readable plain-text table (or per-row custom-content blocks for row-level citations), send as text document. Never truncate; if content exceeds context, tell the user and offer chunking/summarization.

PERSISTENCE in existing libSQL store. Create tables: `conversations` (id, title, created_at, model), `messages` (id, conversation_id, role, content_json, created_at), and `attachments` (id, conversation_id, message_id, filename, mime_type, size_bytes, sha256, local_path, anthropic_file_id nullable, page_count nullable, created_at). Store original bytes on disk under the Electron userData dir (not as BLOBs in SQLite - keeps the DB small); store only metadata + path + optional anthropic_file_id in libSQL. Dedupe by sha256 so the same file across conversations reuses one Files API file_id. Persist assistant message content as the full content_json array so citation blocks (cited_text, document_index, page/char ranges) survive reloads and can be re-rendered as clickable source links.

ATTACHMENT LIFECYCLE. On send: hash file -> check attachments table -> if no live anthropic_file_id and the file qualifies (reused/large), upload via Files API and store file_id; otherwise inline. Because Files-API files you upload cannot be downloaded back, always keep the local copy as the source of truth for re-display and re-send. Add a cleanup job that DELETEs Files API entries when a conversation/attachment is deleted (they persist and count against the 500 GB org quota until deleted).

ATTACHMENT UX in the renderer. Support all three entry points: drag-drop onto the composer, a '+' / paperclip button (file picker), and clipboard paste (capture image/* from the paste event). Show a chip per attachment with filename, type icon, size, and page count for PDFs; validate against limits before send (PDF <=32MB request / <=600 pages, image <=10MB / <=8000px) and surface a clear inline error rather than a 400. Render citations as inline superscripts or highlighted spans that, on click, open the source attachment at the cited page (PDF page_location) or scroll to the cited char range (text char_location).

REDACTION before sending. Run redaction on the EXTRACTED TEXT client-side (never rely on the model to redact). Pipeline: extract text for every format (including running text extraction on PDFs you would otherwise send natively) -> apply regex/NER detectors for emails, phone numbers, SSNs/IDs, credit-card numbers (Luhn), API keys/secrets -> replace with placeholders -> send the redacted text as a plain-text `document` block. Trade-off to surface in the product: redaction forces the text-extraction path, so you lose native-PDF visual understanding and page-level citations on redacted PDFs. Make redaction an explicit per-attachment toggle; default off for trusted local files, on when the user marks a file sensitive. Keep an in-app redaction map so cited_text can be mapped back for the user's own view without re-sending raw PII.

CITATIONS POLICY. Enable citations:{enabled:true} on all document blocks by default (chat-with-your-files is exactly the grounding use case it was built for, and cited_text is free on output tokens). Because citations are all-or-none per request, if any one document in a turn cannot support citations (e.g. a scanned image-only PDF), either drop citations for that whole turn or exclude that document. Do NOT combine citations with output_config.format (structured outputs) - it 400s; if a feature needs strict JSON, run it as a separate non-cited request.

COST/PERF GUARDRAILS. Default model claude-opus-4-8 with streaming + adaptive thinking. Apply cache_control:{ephemeral} to document blocks for multi-turn chat over the same file (huge savings on follow-ups). Use messages.count_tokens (never tiktoken) to pre-estimate a document's token cost and warn the user before sending very large files. For batch/background document processing (e.g. bulk summarization of many files) use the Message Batches API at 50% cost. Downsample images and consider text-extraction over native-PDF when visual fidelity is not needed, to cut image-token spend (native PDF adds per-page image tokens on top of text).
risks: Scanned/image-only PDFs: no extractable text means no citations and empty client-side extraction. Detect low/zero extracted-text ratio and either fall back to native-PDF Q&A without citations or run OCR (the Python pywinauto helper environment could host an OCR step) before sending.
Citations + Structured Outputs are mutually exclusive (400). Any roadmap feature that wants both grounded citations and strict JSON output must split into two API calls.

Files API caveats: beta header required; not ZDR-eligible; uploaded files are non-downloadable (keep local originals); files persist and consume the 500 GB org quota until explicitly deleted (need a cleanup path); ~100 req/min beta rate limit could throttle bulk uploads.

Token/size limits can cause hard failures: 32 MB / 600-page PDF request cap, dense PDFs exhausting context before the page limit, 10 MB image cap. Validate and chunk client-side; never silently truncate (skill guidance) - notify the user and offer options.

Redaction is only as good as the detectors; client-side regex/NER will miss novel PII patterns. Treat it as defense-in-depth, log what was redacted for audit, and make the sensitivity choice explicit to the user rather than implying guaranteed scrubbing.
Storing large file bytes in libSQL/SQLite would bloat the DB and slow it; keep blobs on disk and only metadata in libSQL.
High-resolution images on Opus 4.8 cost up to ~3x older models' image tokens (4784 vs 1568). Without client-side downsampling, image-heavy chats get expensive fast.
